subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The entire chapter is organised around daily-life problems that require collecting, organising, and reading data to answer concrete questions — how many children watch TV for more than two hours, which day had more two-wheelers, how much food was wasted across the week, how many children could have been fed with the wasted food. Students use whole-number operations (counting, addition, subtraction) embedded in pictograph and bar graph reading to solve these real-world questions, making C-4.1 the primary competency in play.

Selecting a scale for a pictograph — whether 1 icon = 5 items, 1 icon = 3 two-wheelers, or a student-chosen scale — is precisely the act of selecting an appropriate method and tool to represent and compute with data efficiently. Multiple tasks require students to choose their own scale and re-draw the same data, directly exercising the competency of matching method to context.

adjunct_competencies.justification: Reading bar graphs and pictographs requires applying the four basic operations on whole numbers: students add icon counts to find totals (total play items, total two-wheelers over three days, total food wasted across the week) and subtract to find differences (fewer two-wheelers on Wednesday than Monday, more hours Sheela spends studying than Raman). These arithmetic operations are in service of data reading, making C-1.3 a genuine cross-CG engagement.

The paper-strip bar graph activity requires students to physically measure the length of each friend's index finger and match it to a cm scale on the graph axis. This is a direct use of the centimetre as a measurement unit and the ruler as the appropriate tool, situating the data representation task within measurement of a body attribute.

The TV-watching data uses fractional hours — half an hour (½ hr), one-and-a-half hours (1½ hr) — as category labels in the frequency table. Students must read and compare these fractional quantities (e.g., deciding whether more children watch for half an hour vs two hours) to answer the table questions, engaging C-1.2's focus on commonly used fractions in daily life.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to organise and interpret data in daily-life contexts by learning to represent information as scaled pictographs and bar graphs, read them to answer comparison and total questions, and choose appropriate scales and tools for the data at hand.
